text: Hello everyone, it's a case of Dermatocyst Palaparoscopic excision.
Dermatocyst is a cyclic growth that is present at birth. It contains structures such as hair, fluid, teeth or skin glands.
This is the planning of the port.
The surgeon utilizes two ipsilateral ports and one contralateral port for access, inserting a ten millimeter zero degree telescope for visualization.

The surgeon follows a baseball diamond concept for port placement, with the telescopic port approximated at the thumb position and the first working port at the left anatomical snuff box. Two ipsilateral and one contralateral ports are visible.
The surgeon inserts a Veress needle perpendicular to the skin surface, performing suction, irrigation, and a hanging drop test to confirm its proper positioning for insufflation.

The surgeon inserts the contact head and checks the gas pressures, ensuring they remain in the single digits until three liters of gas are inside. A ten-millimeter zero-degree telescope is inserted, with a hissing sound confirming the tip's positioning inside the cavity. The telescopic port is entered for diagnostic purposes, and the surgeon visually inspects for any adhesions.
The surgeon inserts the first working port, followed by two ipsilateral ports and one contralateral port, establishing access points for the laparoscopic cystectomy procedure.
This is a case of previous two caesarean section and one being vertical scar to be anticipating a good amount of adhesions to the anteblominal wall with omentum.
The surgeon performs the cystectomy using simple bipolar electrocautery and scissors, manipulating the tissue with precision to achieve the desired surgical outcome.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to coagulate the omentum, ensuring hemostasis to prevent profuse bleeding from the tissue.
The laparoscopic view reveals the anterior wall of the uterus adherent to the abdominal wall, while the pouch of Douglas (POD) appears clean and unobstructed.
It's a good sign to see the POD being not obliterated because the adnexal mobility is too good and we can get the
The surgeon uses traumatic forceps to grasp the ovarian cyst, attempting to negotiate it out of the pouch of Douglas (POD) for adequate exposure during the cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon locates the most prominent part of the cyst wall and uses bipolar cautery to create markings, facilitating the entry of the suction cannula.
The surgeon enters the surgical field with a suction cannula, assisted by traumatic forceps, to facilitate the removal of the ovarian cyst.
The surgeon inserts the suction cannula into the cyst, applying suction to remove the cystic content, which is visible as it enters the cannula.

The surgeon exposes the cyst wall, revealing a clear cyst with visible fat content. Scissors are used to extend the incision, ensuring proper cleavage between the cortex and cyst wall for thorough examination of the cyst contents.
The surgeon extends the incision on the cyst wall using scissors to provide adequate exposure of the cyst wall contents, revealing the internal structure of the cyst.
The surgeon exposes the cyst, revealing its fatty content visible inside the cyst cavity.
The surgeon locates the plane between the exposed cyst wall and the cortex, indicating the separation of these structures.
The surgeon uses a traumatic forceps alongside a non-traumatic Maryland forceps to apply traction on the cyst wall, facilitating the separation of the cyst from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses a Maryland forceps to peel the cyst wall, carefully separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The assistant provides adequate traction while the surgeon uses forceps to peel the cyst wall, visibly advancing the instruments to separate the cyst wall from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon exposes the cyst wall content, revealing a fatty substance without any hair, teeth, or bone. Minimal or no spillage is observed during the cystectomy procedure.
After completing the cystectomy, the surgical field is thoroughly washed. An endobag is used for extraction, with minimal oozing observed during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully peels the thin cortex from the cyst wall, revealing the fatty content of the cyst. Coagulation is avoided at the surgical plane to ensure proper peeling.
The surgeon uses scissors to cut and extract the remaining portion of the cyst wall, which has become thinner. The final segment of the cyst wall is carefully severed and removed.
The surgeon completes the integration of the cyst wall, using scissors to cut the remaining portions with assistance, ensuring the cyst wall is fully separated from surrounding tissues.
A suction device is placed in the anterior part of the uterus to remove surrounding content and prevent spillage into the abdominal cavity.
Tubal spillage is visible on the left side, with fluid leaking from the fallopian tube into the surrounding area.
The surgeon places the cyst wall inside an improvised under bag made from a number seven glove, ensuring containment of the cyst material.
The surgeon demonstrates the use of a glove as an under bag, showing how its opening allows for easy placement and lifting of the cyst wall, which is then held securely at the opening.
The surgeon uses alligator forceps inserted through a ten-millimeter port, assisted by a five-millimeter laparoscopic scope, to extract tissue from the surgical site.

The surgeon uses a 10mm trocar to gently extract the cyst and its wall through the abdominal wall. The glove is fanned over the abdominal wall, and simple atraumatic forceps are used to carefully remove the cyst contents.
This ensures no spillage inside the abdomen cavity and the pectoral cavity.
The surgeon completes the removal of the cyst wall and cystic content, followed by a thorough wash of the abdomen and pelvis using one liter of saline solution to prevent chemical peritonitis.
The surgeon injects 5 cc of ropivacaine into the five millimeter and ten millimeter ports, as well as into the abdominal cavity, to facilitate early recovery and ambulation.
One liter of normal saline is left inside the abdominal cavity to promote better outcomes and reduce the risk of future adhesions.
Thank you for watching.
